And a lot of times when we try to fix churn, you make it worse. I've done this a million times. Like, man, my churn is hiring. I want it to be, I'll give them more stuff. You can set up a third party Facebook conversion API. So one, I don't think, or I don't know for sure that Cartra integrates natively with Facebook, but you can do it with Zapier. You know, so it would be when a purchase happens in Cartra, it would fire Zapier, a Zap, and then that would pass that data to Facebook's conversion API. Nice. Cartra, to Facebook. Yep. So Zapier can hook anything up to Facebook's conversion API, which then makes stuff very difficult to block from an iOS perspective because it's not browser based. It's not cookie based, you know, it's server based. It's going to server to server, which is why I'm always like people are freaking you out about this API or iOS thing way more than they need to be. And so I guess maybe one of the things that we want to address is like, what would we recommend in terms of sales process or tried in the past for rainmaker attracting rainmaker clients? Cool. Okay. I was looking at my monitor to make sure I didn't have any crap on here. Like I'm weird about making sure everything's clean, you know, before we restart with the drawing. So first thing you got to figure out is who do you want? All right. All right. As a client, this is very, very important. So do you want to work with startups? Do you want to work with people in a particular niche? There's no wrong answer to this, by the way, but it's who do you want? And then you got to figure out what do they want? And so this is like of major importance. Right. What is it that your who wants more than anything? And it's really easy to be like, oh, everybody just wants more money, but it's a lot deeper than that depending on who you're talking to. You know, if you're talking to a business owner that's already doing very well, an incremental increase in profit may not be nearly as appealing as a greater sense of time freedom and a diminishment of overwhelm. If you're talking to a startup, then of course, more money consistently is going to be significantly more important than that sort of thing. Right. So you got to figure out what they want, you know, and then the ultimate offer for this stuff is I will get you results. Or I will pay you as irresistible. All right. So this is how it worked very, very well for us. And the pitch was, hey, listen, we will make more than you pay us or we will pay you the difference plus an additional 20%. So if they paid us five grand and we only brought in four grand and we got to write them a check for that thousand bucks plus 20%, which is 1200, which if you think about it, isn't really that bad for us. Right. So we didn't always win. By the way, sometimes we would take the wrong client or we would think we would know what to do and we would be wrong. That happens. But if you make that type of an offer and in that situation, you're not actually giving them back and this, you know, with this hypothetical example, five grand plus an extra 20% on top of that, you're giving them the difference between how much they paid you and how much you made for them plus 20%. So in that example, if they gave us five and we only made them four, then I would have to write them a check for 1200, which means I actually got to keep 3800, which still ain't that bad. And they came out okay either way. So this is an irresistible offer. However, going to, just like generally to any market and saying, I will get you results, that's too broad. Right. So it's very important to clarify what those results are that you can get and who you're talking to. So if I were to do this again, for example, which we probably won't because I'm bad at management and I've just had enough of doing it, you know, y'all can do it for a while. I'm tired. We spent all of 2021 doing this stuff and it wore me out. I wouldn't say the exact same thing, you know, I would say if you're a, and actually I would clarify a little bit more because I'll charge more. All right. So I would say if your business is making over seven figures in profit every month, I will measurably and significantly increase your revenue or you don't pay. As a matter of fact, I'll take it one step further. I would generate more than you pay me in a 30 day period or I will return the difference to you plus an extra 20%. So it's physically impossible for your interaction with me not to be profitable. And then I would make them apply and then I would only take the ones I thought that I could get a good job for or do a good job for rather. That pitch works great. You know, the trick is being able to do this and the ability to do this is reliant on two things. Maybe more than two things. Let's see. Number one, the business assets. All right. So what does the business have in place? So what the dude also is talking to earlier, like he would be an easy win, assuming that everything was, you know, was true, which I believe was true by the way, but let's say the average person pays 99 bucks, you know, and they've got an email list of freaking 50,000 people or whatever. It would be very, very simple for me to be like, okay, it's 5,000 bucks. And I'm going to build you three email campaigns or however many and those email campaigns are just going to be the ones I just outlined, right? You know, with the same offer structures that I just outlined, because I already know that they have a sales page that works already know they have a working offer. That's simple to do. So it's the business assets. And the second thing is going to be the average customer value. So someone had a list of say 50,000 people and their average customer is a dollar. It's going to be very difficult for me to make them more than in this illustrative example, the five grand, you know, was like, good Lord, I'm going to have to convert like a shitload of these people, 5,000 sales out of this. This is ridiculous. I don't want to do that. But if their average customer value is 100 bucks, then that's going to be pretty simple. All I got to do is get 50 people out of 50,000. That's very simple for me to do. And I've, I've hit the target. So that's that second thing that you need in order to win. And then the third thing is your own skill. And it's really not your magical ability to get these results because it's really very formulaic, you know, but it's more of a skill to keep digging and find these, the tools that you've got at your disposal to get the results for them. But that's exactly how you do it. And it's a very easy pitch, you know, and it's a very good positioning for you as dramatically different from other people. What we did, what I would not do it over is we went for a monthly retainer. I wouldn't do that. I would just go for one off jobs. And then if they wanted to do something else, then okay, you know, we'll do another one for you or whatever, but I wouldn't go for the monthly. That's a thousand times harder to sell. I would do it as a project based thing. And it's so funny because when we first started selling this stuff, we would sell like 20 K packages of this. And everybody was fine and nobody was a pain in the ass. That one person, Tyler, that one lady that just never did what we wanted her to do. We still made her money though. But once we switched to monthly, it was, we became the help, you know, and it became almost like, let us get rid of these guys as soon as possible because it's a bill. So I would do it as a one off thing if I were you. Hopefully that was like a long ass answer to the question, but ultimately is that's your offer. Yeah, I think it's a solid offer and they kind of helped answer the question about, you know, do I niche down or do I work with anybody because you seem to be able to help anybody? So what it'll do is it'll wait 30 minutes and we'll see if they bought. So this is like a question mark. Hey, did you buy? If they bought, then E stands for end. If they did not buy, so this stands for no right here. What we'll do is we'll send an email, which is like from a real person. And then we'll also queue up a text. Which is from a real person. Just to clarify how we do this, and I'm certainly not an attorney, you know, no one should listen to me about anything ever under any circumstances. We send our texts manually. So it's peer to peer. It's not auto dialing. It's not automated. So what happens in this particular instance, and some of you all might have experienced this, is you start to order, you don't, and then you get a text from Nora, my daughter, and is like, hey, looks like you started doing a role, but you didn't. Do you have any questions? Let me know. And so even though you're in the midst of a deadline sequence, doing all of this is very smart, you know, and it makes you more money. It's not worth doing on a $4 offer, but it's worth doing on a $100 offer or more, for sure. Nice. All right. Thank you for that. So my wife and I have been doing online business stuff for probably about eight years now, and we started with blogging and they kind of propelled on. And we're at the stage now where we serve a specific niche, primarily moms or females, getting started in online business. We have a whole bunch of low ticket offers. We have courses, we have challenges, templates, things like that, ebooks. And we run significant, well, I mean, we run ads not significant. We've run $41,000 so far this year off and on. We've started to ramp them up again. But we are in this, I'm in this struggle of understanding how to kind of engineer that customer journey because we have so many different little low ticket products. And it's not exactly step one, step two, step three, like synchronize. They can kind of choose our own adventure as they're going through the content that we teach. I've always struggled to figure out how to craft that customer journey where I see kind of how your click sequences and deadline sequences kind of follow a bit of a pattern. And I've never really figured out how to craft that customer journey when there's not exactly product A leads to product B type of situation. Well, you go by what sells best. You know, so if like you're waiting, let's say you got, what's your main thing that whenever you do run ads, what's the thing that they buy from the ads? Well, we, most of our ads go to free legions. We've not had a ton of success driving straight to a sale through our ads. That's our hot seller that we drive sale, drive traffic straight to the sale. Cool. Okay. So number one, I would have a sequence for the printables because that seems to be the best seller, right? Man, this is so hard to write with a mouse. What's the fourth one? Oh, goodness gracious. Probably at that point is going after self publishing on Amazon. Cool. And that's where, you know, nothing specifically fall. Like you don't have to do one before you do the other. So it's been tricky for me. And I've always thought chronologically in the journey, but you're going from best seller down to least best seller. I can see. Yeah. Cause I just wanted you to get the money, you know, so like you're running lead gen and we know this sells best as a second best as a third best. This is fourth best and it's like, let's put our best foot forward. And I would build campaigns for all of these, you know, and it's always going to be the same thing. Quick sequence to with a deadline sequence. Yeah. Each campaign. They're just saying, check it out. If they check it out, then, hey, you know, it's going to go away here. You better go get it. And if they don't check it out, then you don't send them the deadline sequence, which brings us now into the super system concept. You know, and so in Rainmaker, I kind of outline the super system concept and it works like this. So within, so let's say like this is your opt in, right? Is there someone opts in for whatever, the free report about whatever, the free report about how to read free reports, free report helps you get more free reports. All right. So now they're going to get a quick sequence to this thing, you know, and if they click on it, then they're going to get in the deadline sequence. All right. So, and let's just pretend these are the only products you've got. And so now they've gone through all of these and they're at the end. So now what you can do is you can start again, you can run them through again, and then it'll look and it'll be like, okay, for the printable thing, did they click? So you can set up your active campaign or infusion software, whatever, and look and see if they clicked here. And if the answer is no, they never clicked it, then we're going to send them the printable campaign again. You mean if they never clicked it on the first time they went through that sequence at the top? Yeah. If they never clicked here, then we could send them the same damn thing again, because it's been long enough that they've forgotten it, you know, so we can run them through. If the answer is yes, they did click, all right. So then we can now be like, okay, what about the blog course? I see. And it's going to be the same logic. Did you click? Did they click? And, you know, I know my drawing is horrible. And if the answer is no, then now we send them back through the blog course again. But if they did click, then we do the same thing for the 30 day course. So you can actually get double the mileage out of this thing, which is pretty sweet. Yeah, that's really smart. I like that a lot. Do you do much in terms of segmenting, meaning, hey, this person because they opted in for this thing, they're probably not interested in that offer. So let me send them down this path. Like we've gotten overly convoluted, I feel, over the years. Do you mess around a whole lot with segmenting, or do you kind of just run them all through that engine one by one? Run them all through everything. Yeah. Because I don't pretend to know what they want. You know, I mean, like, it's the internet for crying out loud. Like they could be interested in, you know, Ford trucks one day, and then Chevy Camaro's the next day. Like, I don't know, you know, we're just going to see because the sending of the emails isn't really a big deal, especially when you follow the structure. So if you do click the deadline sequence, the quick sequences are always so like mild, you know what I mean? Like read this, if you want to learn about creating a blog, here's a video I made for you, check it out, you know. And so if they never click that, they're not going to be getting like all those hardcore pitches about it is never going to trigger the deadline sequences. So at the surface level, it doesn't really, you're never really being very annoying. You're kind of like, Hey, you want to check this out? Pretty neat. Okay, no. Hey, man, would you want some water? No, all right, cool. Hey, dude, you need a calculator? All right, it's not like by this calculator, you're going to die. You know, the only time you do that is if they're like, Yes, as a matter of fact, I am interested in calculator, then you do the, you know, the countdown sequence stuff. So between each one of those sequence pairs, like going from the printables to the blog, do you create like a, so the cool off period between from one to the other is like, you just go, you kick them straight through from one to the other. Two days, typically, I'll give it a rest for two days. And then, yeah, because they're not nearly as interested in us as we hope they are, and they're not paying as much attention. You know, so we got to, we really need to strike while the iron is hot. You know, and that's why I'm such a big proponent of those two sequence strategies, click sequence and deadline sequence. So if they click now, we're going to really hone in with that deadline, you know, so like, we guys are on my list, you've probably seen it, I'm like, Hey, go watch this video about the thing. And you go watch that video, you're going to get at least, you know, two emails a day for two days and then six emails on the last day for the deadline sequence. It's templated framework, you know, same thing every time, like you all can pick up my pattern real fast. If you watch it, you're like, Oh, this dude is literally following the exact same pattern every time. It's because it works. That's smart. No, I like it. I've had that thought before. You know, like all your other products just glue them together. Like it's not going to break anything. You know, you can't like, every day that you're going without sending the database and offer or is a day that you're making less money than you would have made had you sent an offer to the database. Yeah, that's that's a very true statement. Cool. It makes sense. Thanks. Yeah. Yeah. And then when you do this, by the way, now like it makes the cost per cost per lead less relevant. So when you start tracking, you know, how much did I spend on leads in February? Last question I have on for you, if you don't mind is we have been so focused on the front end and in the back of my mind, you know, we hear all the high ticket, you know, people, you know, saying you have to go high ticket, we've done quite well, sticking with the low ticket, but you know, I'm definitely antsy to see what it, what else is better. So I guess what do you have any, I don't know, mindset advice, or you've gone from doing less client work and more of the group coaching thing. And we are trying to pick out what our back end offer is going to be. We have all these leads who have bought everything, or all these customers who bought everything we have to offer. And there's literally no other way for them to give us money, but they still know, like and trust us, we don't know what to do with from a recurring revenue perspective to kind of allow the people who really like us to continue to support us. What would you say to somebody who's struggling there? Well, there's, there's first the high ticket thing. So I want to address it. It's cool. You got to have the, the personality for it. I don't, you know, like we've made a lot of money at high ticket, but I don't like having salespeople. I can't stand that. I don't like having to deal with all of that sort of stuff. That's why we don't do it. There's nothing wrong with the model. It's never failed. It's just a matter of personal preference. It's like, man, I don't want to mess with this damn having to deal with this stuff. You know, it's too much of a hassle. So there, when you see the high ticket webinars, it's always sort of ask yourself, okay, do I want to be in a situation where I'm having to manage humans and dealing with that? It's, it's not particularly fun for me, but it might be for you. Like other people don't mind it. You know, I'm, I'm awful at it. So there's that for recurring what has, what made me the most money with the least effort was physically shipped newsletter every month and two group calls a month. And when I, and so this, this goes back to the original conversation from earlier this morning about, Hey, every time I tried to fix churn on these things, I ended up making it worse. So we had, I think about 15% churn with that program. Which is relatively normal at that price point, you know, it would be terrible for software or something, but for information, which is largely elective, you know, and I mean, it's not like a tool you have to have, it's pretty normal churn would be that 15%. So I started thinking like, okay, man, I'm going to add more stuff, you know, and as we added more stuff, our stick rate got worse. So the, the average customer value of the continuity program, where I just sent them a physical ship newsletter, and there were two calls a month, that was $2,305, according to Bear Metrics, which was a little software thing that could tell you your average customer value over time. I tried to make it better by adding like daily calls and everything I've ever done in a member's area and all this stuff, the average customer value plummeted to about $1,200. Yeah, so if you're going to do continuity, the trick is don't give them a ton of stuff, and it's not like you're holding out on them, it's because that they get overwhelmed with it. So newsletter and group calls works real good, you know, that's what I've noticed. Nice. Yeah, that helps a lot. We've always been hesitant to do like communities or Facebook groups and things like that, because we know how much time that can suck out of your day and how much drama that can create. So it's nice to hear a good sticky continuity that's not fully community focused. Yeah, so I'm terrible at community, like just I am, you know, because I'm a weird dude that sits in this little room all day in the dark. It really is, it's like I'm in under interrogation all day. So I've got like three of these El Gato lights that are just shining directly at me, and then there's no windows open and there's no other lights in the room. Because like, it only breaks every time you mess with it. I'm like, no, this time will be different. Awesome. You, so that was the last of the hot seats and we have about 15 minutes left. So you had mentioned that you take clients with the return on investment guarantee and we had two questions about that. The first one is, is what happens after 30 days and we're not able to get a result because the client isn't listening to us? Well, it's, we still got to honor it, you know, like you can't, I mean, I guess you could fight them on it or something, but it's, when we do that, we like do stuff, you know, so it's not just advice. And that's one thing that, that I prefer over consulting, like consulting is cool because it sounds cool and everything, but most clients aren't going to do what you tell them to do. It doesn't matter how much they pay you, by the way, they just ain't going to do it. So in hours, we did it for them. You know, it was like, okay, here's what we are going to do for you. We're going to build this thing and it's going to make money. So we didn't really have that if they didn't listen to us problem where we would have problems would be number one, we were just wrong. And you know, that was pretty rare, not because we're great or anything, but it's just really not hard to be right with this. Like, okay, they've got a database, they're selling this thing, it converts it this much, they never mail the database, you know, maybe we'll mail the database, like really basic stuff. So the places where we would get hurt would be if someone had like, let's say a database of 14,000 people or something. And what they neglected to tell us was the database was like nine years old, you know, and they never opened the emails ever, like we had stuff like that happen and we'd give the money back, like, are you gonna do? But it was very few and far between, you know. And then we had that one, like we had one lady that told us she had all this, you know, here are my numbers and here was my best month and all that kind of stuff, we just took it at point blank. So we reproduced everything that she said had worked and absolutely nothing happened. And she's like, nothing happened, you guys suck. And I was like, man, it's weird, we did everything that you said worked. And then because we had access to the CRM, it turns out that none of the data that we had gotten was true at all, like the best month, like the things that were reportedly sold were not sold at all, like nothing happened. So, you know, some it's possible for people to hose you, but it's pretty unlikely, you know, I mean, it's pretty rare. And then the only other second part to that question is what if the sales cycle for the specific product or whatever is longer. So six months, for example, how would you be able to guarantee results when you're working with them in a shorter period of time? Oh, I wouldn't. Yeah, I wouldn't take that deal. So that's the thing my father told me this in relation to, to the dating, he was like, just because you can, doesn't mean you should son. And that was not listened to. And I paid heftily. Just because you can take a client doesn't mean you should. So we would turn down clients. And to this day, I would turn them down, you know, like if someone's like, okay, I have this sales cycle, it's six months, like I don't want to be freaking on the hook for six months. This is ridiculous. No, I'm going to pass. There's so much demand for, for Rainmaker stuff, not like the brand or anything. I mean, it's not a brand. I don't have a trademark or anything. But people who can actually make the phone ring or make the cash register ring more accurately, that you can refuse clients. It's not like you have to take them all. We did a test, I remember this, and this was to strangers, you know, so it wasn't like, Oh, okay, I work for you for like this, everybody knows you. We did a test to scale where I had a sales manager and he hired 10 people. And I was like, screw it, you know, let's just see what happens. If we book appointments for these 10 people all week long. And man, we got something like 200 appointments booked in a week on three days of advertising. It's, it was a very, very hot offer to go to the market and say this, I will do this, you know, this is my promise to you. You just don't want to take them all, this clients, you know, so that's very important. Like don't take the ones you can't help, take the obvious no brainer winners, and then make it an irresistible offer to where it's pretty affordable for them. And that sets you up for victory. And then that sets you up for repeat business, where they'll be like, Oh, cool, you know, you want, what else you got? And tell them, well, and build this other thing for you if you want, keep on going. Awesome. Okay. So Steve says, we have a client who's a corporate speaker, we're starting to explore building leads and getting him more speaking engagements through ads, he works with larger corporations, how could YouTube and Google ads be targeted to reach the right audience given it's a corporate decision maker audience. That's gonna be a tough one. Okay. So he's going to or she the individual, it's you try so hard to be politically correct, you sound dumb. The individual is going to have to understand that the cost to acquire that client's going to be a little higher than like, if you were going to a single decision maker. So one very simple thing to do is if you've got a, let's say a list, hang on. If you've got like a dream 100 list, you know, we're okay, these are all the companies I want to get this person booked with, well, you're going to know their physical locations. So Google and Facebook, both will allow you to serve advertising based on the physical location of an individual down to a one mile radius, if not shorter, I can't remember how tightly targeted you can get, but it's so cheap, you know, so if let's say we wanted to get, I don't know, four dealership and Sinita's four down the street from me and I wanted them as a client. I could for a week serve like to saturate the ever living hell out of every adult over the age of 40, you know, because I'm just assuming that's going to be the decision maker age like arbitrarily pulling an age out of the air, right? Every adult over the age of 40 that is within a one mile radius of insinitas for it, I could saturate them with just content for me knowing that next week, they're going to start getting calls from people that are going to try to pitch them on book in me. So now they're very familiar with me, you know, so that's one simple way to do it. And that you could even retarget those viewers with direct call to action ads, but it's in a corporate environment, you're going to need to like human interaction, you know what I mean. So I would have a person calling and reaching out trying to get to the decision maker after it. We call it a surgical strike campaign. And we originally came up with the concept for HVAC people and door to door roofers where they would go in and like after a storm or whatever, where you just saturate a specific geographic location with advertising before the salespeople show up because now they're familiar with the brand before the salespeople show up makes it a whole lot easier. Very cool. That is a very bad ass technique, by the way. And then last question was about that poll return on the pessimistic guarantee. Did we ever take into consideration ad spend? No, it's just we're going to make you more than you pay us, you know, and we didn't have like a lot of if anybody was ever weird, where it felt weird, where they were like, okay, I want to make sure that this is going to happen. It's like, okay, this is a pretty good indication that you ain't got your shit together and I don't need to be taking you as a client. You know what I mean? So the whole premise of this is it allows you to have so much demand that you can say no, and you should say no, unless it's perfect, right? Because otherwise, everybody is under stress. So if it's somebody who didn't like have any money, for example, even though they said they were profitable or whatever, but like the cost was an issue to them, we would like, we don't want to, we don't want this, you know, we're going to pass, it just doesn't feel right, you know, it's us, it's not you. If they were super nitpicky about that, then we would pass because it was a clear indicator they're looking for a way to claim that guarantee. We are always just looking for people like, yeah, man, okay, sweet, let's go. You know, and that's who we would take. And sometimes we'd screw up, you know, but if we had to do it again, we wouldn't screw up, it'd be like, no, we ain't gonna take you and we wouldn't do it monthly. That was what made it really, really difficult, you know, was the monthly thing, because people don't like paying a bill like that. They want to get, they want to lower that expense as much as possible. So think about the premise, you know, you got a person like, okay, they've got a 50,000 person list, their average order value is 200, you know, there's stuff that you learn during Rainmaker on how to assess whether or not they're going to be good client or not. And if it looks like they are, they pitch them something for like five or 10 grand, where it's like a no brainer like, okay, yeah, I can probably make this person 10 G's, you know, or more. And that's the entire basis of the conversation. And you just tell them exactly how you're going to do it, you know, oh, well, I'm going to create this campaign for you, we're going to send it and it's probably going to make the 10 grand back plus some and if it works, you can just keep on using as much as you want. There you go, the end, you know, Well, the good clients usually sounded like you, like they knew that there was money there. There was a lot of agreements along the way. All the ones that were talking about anything were not great. Yeah. Never afraid to say no, you know, like just no should be the first response. Like, I don't know, because you really are taking a gamble with them. And Manor sales manager was so good at this. And really what what made the whole thing suck actually was sales people, because they didn't quite understand how to figure out whether or not a client was going to be a pain in the ass or they're going to be good, you know. So if it was like me or Tyler or the sales manager closing that, then we would know, but like a sales person didn't have quite the experience to be like, okay, this guy's going to be a pain in the ass. They just want to get that commission. But yeah, like our sales manager, when the person would start like, hey, you know, how do I know blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. I've been I've worked with other people and they were horrible. The sales managers response would usually be something like, you know what it is, is like we're on a first date right now and you're telling me how horrible your ex was. And I'm not very interested at this point and going on a second date, like, we're the one taking the gamble here, not you, you know, what do you want to do? And then that would, maybe we need to straighten them out or it wouldn't, but there's just no shortage of demand. So you really are in the, you know, you're in a pretty good position when you can deliver the results and walk away if it doesn't work for you. Solid. We'll take one more question. Joshua says a question about lead ads because they're iOS proof and because they're cheap, why not use them for all quick lead gens on Facebook or free ebooks, resources, et cetera, rather than driving folks to a landing page to get them to sign up for the free fee? It's a good question. There's no reason not to unless you don't have the mental stamina and knowledge of how to track whether or not they're actually going to consume the lead magnet. So what we found is a freaking lead, lead formats are very, very profitable. And we should probably turn them on for our evergreen version of this, by the way, we're paying, I think on average, like six bucks a lead right now for this. I know they'd be cheaper if we were doing lead form. So what we found is number one, a pretty damn, they're pretty good, excuse me, lead form ads. So what typically happens and why most people will drop the ball with these and why I haven't turned them on for this offer is because I've been a little lazy, but you'll have your ad, right? So your ad and it goes to Facebook lead form. Now, after they've submitted their information on the Facebook lead format, conceptually, they can actually go to your website and like click on the lead magnet, but very few people do because the way the system works is like kind of hard. So what you need to have here is a quick sequence. And it's really smart if the quick sequence has a little bit of a deadline to it, like, okay, so really, we're going to call this a countdown sequence. All right, here's the thing that you opted in for. The link is going to expire in four days or whatever. The more of this you have, the better chance of success you're going to have with it. We found that we would end up with about 30% of these guys would actually click and this was with a two day click sequence and then I never built it out to be any fancier. But if you don't have that, then this is going to be like 5% of your guys actually go and check out the lead magnet and stuff. So you have to really do exactly what we do in the checklists, you know, which is like, okay, do you have a sequence to get them to go and consume the damn thing? And then do you have a sequence that's triggered after they consume the damn thing? And then do you have a next for that? And if you don't have all that built, then you're your host. So the advantage of Facebook lead ads is they're cheaper. The disadvantage is you have to build a thing that drives them to go consume your lead magnet slash sales message. Whereas on a website, you don't they're going to see the sales message immediately upon off the end. So that's your that's your difference. So if you don't have this part, you're you're doomed. I should write that because it's a dude actually that would solve so many problems for me.
